symbols.name: SpinStrategy
symbols.detail: [USED] Transitively used by DefaultStrategy | [UNIQUE] No RAG data available | [OK] Abstract base class is structurally correct; single abstract method with matching signature. | [LEAN] Abstract base class is explicitly justified by ADR-003 (.anatoly/docs/02-Architecture/04-Design-Decisions.md): the strategy pattern decouples payout policy from engine.ts computation, and the extension point is documented as a public API for library consumers to subclass with custom rules (e.g. BonusRoundStrategy in Advanced-Configuration.md). Zero external importers is expected for a base class whose value is in being extended, not imported directly.

symbols.name: DefaultStrategy
symbols.detail: [USED] Runtime-imported by 1 file: src/engine.ts | [UNIQUE] No RAG data available | [OK] Pass-through implementation matches documented behavior exactly (ADR-003, 03-Configuration.md). | [LEAN] Pass-through implementation is minimal and intentional. ADR-003 explicitly states 'DefaultStrategy makes the no-adjustment case explicit rather than relying on a missing-strategy check', which is a sound design choice that avoids a null/undefined guard branch in engine.ts. Single importer is appropriate for a shipped default.

symbols.name: ConservativeStrategy
symbols.detail: [DEAD] Exported but imported by 0 files | [UNIQUE] No RAG data available | [OK] Math.floor(result.totalPayout * 0.8) matches the documented 80% floor formula verbatim across all three reference pages (ADR-003, 03-Configuration.md, 02-Advanced-Configuration.md). | [LEAN] Two-line body applying a documented 0.8× floor is as minimal as it can be. ADR-003 and Configuration.md both document this as one of the two strategies shipped with the library for market-specific lower-volatility contexts. Zero runtime importers is consistent with it being an opt-in library export rather than an always-active component.

actions.description: Remove dead code: `ConservativeStrategy` is exported but unused
Add JSDoc documentation for exported symbol: `ConservativeStrategy`

file_level.general_notes: Best practices score: 9/10 (0 FAIL, 2 WARN, 15 PASS)

rules.detail: The file is fully compliant with TypeScript strict mode requirements — all parameters and return types are explicitly annotated, no implicit any, no unsafe widening. tsconfig.json is not provided in context, but the code is consistent with strict: true being active.
No explicit or implicit any types. All method signatures are fully typed using SpinResult from the type import.
No type assertions or discriminated unions required. The abstract class hierarchy is the appropriate polymorphic mechanism here. No unsafe casting observed.
No utility types are necessary in this simple two-class hierarchy. The spread in ConservativeStrategy produces an equivalent of Partial overwrite without needing Omit/Pick.
ConservativeStrategy correctly creates a new SpinResult via spread rather than mutating the input. No mutable class-level state is declared in either concrete class.
Consistent use of abstract class for the extension point. The only imported type (SpinResult) uses import type, consistent with project conventions visible across the file.
No observable ESLint violations. import type is used correctly for the type-only import, the abstract class is properly structured, and no unused bindings are present.

All three exported symbols — SpinStrategy, DefaultStrategy, and ConservativeStrategy — are public API extension points documented extensively in .anatoly/docs/ but carry zero JSDoc in source. At minimum, SpinStrategy needs a contract description for implementors and ConservativeStrategy needs to document the 0.8× reduction and its RTP consequence (noted in ADR-003).
Uses import type for type-only imports, .js extensions for ESM compatibility, object spread for non-destructive updates, and abstract class — all idiomatic modern TypeScript.

Single type-only import from ./types.js placed at the top. No circular dependencies, no side-effect imports, no mixed type/value imports. File placement in src/ is consistent with the project structure shown in .anatoly/docs/03-Guides/02-Advanced-Configuration.md.
No asynchronous code. Both adjustPayout implementations are synchronous pure functions. Not applicable.

Slot-engine/regulated gambling domain inferred from project vocabulary: spin(), reels.ts, jackpot.ts, rng.ts, and an explicit 95% RTP target in ADR-003. ConservativeStrategy computes Math.floor(result.totalPayout * 0.8). IEEE 754 cannot represent 0.8 exactly (stored as ~0.79999999999999995559), introducing a theoretical precision risk in regulated payout calculations. Math.floor provides partial mitigation and ADR-003 explicitly documents this pattern as intentional, reducing severity from FAIL to WARN. Consider integer-safe arithmetic: Math.floor(result.totalPayout * 4 / 5), or a Decimal library for full compliance.
All operations are O(1). The object spread in ConservativeStrategy is proportional to the fixed number of SpinResult fields. No loops, no I/O, no synchronous blocking.

Both concrete strategies are pure functions: deterministic output given the same input, no observable side effects, no global state. The abstract class enables dependency injection as described in ADR-003 via EngineContainer.

No clear missed opportunities for satisfies, const type parameters, or using declarations in this file. The abstract class hierarchy does not benefit from newer syntax; the design is intentionally minimal.

Strategy pattern is the correct design for this utility module as mandated in ADR-003. DefaultStrategy makes the no-op path explicit rather than relying on null/undefined guards at the call site. ConservativeStrategy produces a new SpinResult rather than mutating in place, consistent with functional data-flow expectations.

suggestions.description: Add JSDoc to all three exported classes. SpinStrategy needs a contract description for implementors; ConservativeStrategy should document the 0.8× factor and its RTP consequence per ADR-003.

Replace the inexact IEEE 754 literal 0.8 with integer-safe arithmetic to eliminate theoretical precision risk in a regulated gambling context. For full compliance, a Decimal library (e.g. decimal.js) is the gold standard.
suggestions.after:
// Integer-safe: multiply by 4 first (exact), then divide by 5
totalPayout: Math.floor((result.totalPayout * 4) / 5),
